sections.title: Unit 1 – SC Alcohol Laws & Legal Responsibility

contents.text: In this unit we will discuss the following topics:

paragraphs: South Carolina alcohol laws are enforced at the state level. These laws control who can sell alcohol, how it is served, and when service is allowed. As a server, you must follow both state law and your workplace policies. Failing to follow the law can lead to penalties for you and your employer. Your role directly impacts legal compliance and public safety.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol sales in South Carolina require proper licensing. Businesses must hold a valid permit before selling beer, wine, or liquor. Different permits apply depending on the type of alcohol and business model. For example, restaurants, bars, and retail stores may have different requirements. The South Carolina Department of Revenue, through Alcohol Beverage Licensing (ABL), is responsible for issuing the permits and licenses used for alcohol sales in the state. Always work under a valid license. Serving alcohol without proper authorization is illegal.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Permits must be displayed or available at the business location. Inspectors and law enforcement may check licenses at any time. You should know where permits are kept and confirm your workplace is operating legally. If a business is found operating without proper licensing, it can face serious consequences, including fines or shutdown.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol laws also control hours of sale. These hours can vary depending on the type of alcohol and location. Some areas may have additional local restrictions. You must stop service when required by law. Continuing to serve outside legal hours can result in penalties. Always follow your employer’s cutoff times and closing procedures.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Only approved businesses can sell certain types of alcohol. For example, liquor sales are more restricted than beer and wine. Some locations may not be allowed to sell specific products at all. Understanding what your establishment is licensed to sell helps prevent illegal service. Never assume all alcohol types are treated the same.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Servers can be held responsible for illegal alcohol service. This includes selling to underage individuals or serving outside permitted hours. Even if a manager is present, you are still accountable for your actions. Knowing the law helps you make correct decisions and avoid personal risk. Always ask questions if you are unsure.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Law enforcement and regulatory agencies have the authority to inspect alcohol-serving businesses. These inspections can happen during normal operations. Officers may check identification practices, observe service, and review permits. The South Carolina Law Enforcement Division (SLED) may be involved in investigations, compliance checks, and other enforcement actions related to alcohol violations. Cooperation during inspections is required. Interfering with an inspection can lead to additional penalties.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Local governments may set additional rules for alcohol sales. These can include zoning restrictions or local ordinances. Your employer should train you on any location-specific rules. However, it is your responsibility to follow them during service. Always be aware that rules may differ depending on where you work.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: In South Carolina, it is illegal to sell or serve alcohol to anyone under 21 years old. This applies to all alcohol types, including beer, wine, and liquor. As a server, you must verify age before completing a sale. Failing to do so can lead to legal consequences. Preventing underage sales is one of your most important responsibilities.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: In South Carolina, the safest and most consistent practice is to check identification for every person before selling or serving alcohol, regardless of how old they appear. Appearance is not a reliable way to determine legal age. If a person cannot provide acceptable identification, you must refuse service. Applying the same process to everyone helps protect you and your business from underage alcohol sale violations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: South Carolina law allows enforcement actions against both the server and the business for underage sales. Even if the mistake is unintentional, you can still be held responsible. This is why careful ID checking is critical. One poor decision can lead to serious consequences for your job and the establishment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Acceptable identification typically includes government-issued photo IDs. These may include driver’s licenses or other official identification cards. IDs must be current and not expired. You should never accept altered, damaged, or suspicious identification. If something seems wrong, do not complete the sale.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Underage individuals may attempt to use fake or borrowed IDs. It is your job to watch for signs of fraud. Look closely at the photo, physical details, and overall condition of the ID. If the ID does not match the person presenting it, you must refuse service. Trust your judgment and follow policy.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: It is also illegal for adults to purchase alcohol for minors. This is sometimes called a “third-party sale.” If you suspect someone is buying alcohol for an underage person, you must refuse the sale. Pay attention to group behavior and conversations. Your responsibility includes preventing indirect access to alcohol.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: If you refuse a sale, stay calm and professional. Clearly explain that you cannot serve alcohol without valid identification or due to legal concerns. Do not argue or escalate the situation. If needed, involve a manager. Refusing service properly helps reduce conflict and keeps you in compliance with the law.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Law enforcement may conduct compliance checks using underage individuals. These operations test whether businesses follow ID laws. You will not be warned ahead of time. Treat every customer the same by checking ID before selling or serving alcohol. Consistent behavior is the best way to pass compliance checks.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

text.instruction_text: Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: South Carolina law prohibits serving alcohol to anyone who is intoxicated. This applies to all alcohol types and all licensed establishments. As a server, you must recognize when a customer has had too much to drink. Continuing to serve an intoxicated person increases safety risks and can lead to legal consequences for both you and your employer.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Intoxication affects judgment, coordination, and reaction time. Customers may not always realize how impaired they are. It is your responsibility to observe behavior and make decisions based on what you see. Do not rely on what a customer says about their condition. Focus on clear signs and act early to prevent further intoxication.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Once a customer shows clear signs of intoxication, you must stop serving alcohol. This decision should be based on observed behavior, not personal opinion. Waiting too long increases risk. Acting at the right time helps prevent accidents, injuries, and legal issues tied to over-service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Refusing service can be difficult, but it is required when a customer is intoxicated. Stay calm and respectful. Use simple, clear language when explaining your decision. Do not argue or place blame. If needed, involve a manager for support. Handling the situation professionally helps reduce conflict and keeps you compliant.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: South Carolina DUI laws make it illegal to operate a vehicle while impaired by alcohol. Impairment can occur before a person feels “drunk.” As a server, your actions can help prevent impaired driving. Stopping service at the right time reduces the chance a customer will leave and drive while impaired.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Serving an intoxicated person can lead to legal consequences. Both the server and the business may be held responsible. These consequences may include fines, license actions, or other penalties. Even one mistake can have serious impact. This is why consistent monitoring and decision-making are essential during every shift.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Customers may try to continue drinking after you refuse service. Stay firm in your decision. Do not serve additional alcohol once intoxication is clear. If the situation escalates, follow workplace procedures and involve management. Your safety and the safety of others should always come first.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Helping intoxicated customers leave safely is part of responsible service. Encourage safe options such as rides from friends, taxis, or rideshare services. Do not allow an intoxicated person to drive if you can intervene safely. Taking action at this stage helps prevent DUI incidents and protects lives.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: South Carolina law addresses the presence of concealed weapons in alcohol-serving establishments. Certain locations that sell alcohol for on-premises consumption may restrict firearms. These rules are designed to reduce risk in environments where alcohol is consumed. As a server, you are not expected to enforce weapon laws directly, but you should understand basic restrictions and follow your workplace policies.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Businesses have the right to set rules about weapons on their property. Some establishments may prohibit firearms entirely. These rules are often posted at entrances. If a location restricts weapons, customers must follow those rules. As a staff member, you should be aware of your workplace policy and report concerns to management.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Your role is to observe and report, not to confront. If you notice a weapon or feel unsafe, notify a manager immediately. Do not attempt to handle the situation yourself. Safety is the priority. Following proper reporting procedures helps reduce risk and ensures the situation is handled appropriately.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Law enforcement officers have authority to enter and inspect alcohol-serving establishments. They may check permits, observe operations, and respond to incidents. You must cooperate with officers during any interaction. Providing accurate information and following instructions supports compliance and avoids additional issues.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Regulatory agencies in South Carolina oversee alcohol laws and licensing. These agencies work with law enforcement to enforce rules. They may conduct inspections or investigations when needed. Understanding that enforcement can happen at any time helps you stay prepared and consistent in your actions.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: If a situation involving a weapon becomes unsafe or escalates, follow workplace procedures immediately. This may include alerting management or contacting law enforcement. Do not ignore warning signs. Acting quickly and appropriately helps protect staff and customers from potential harm.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: You should also understand your legal limits as a server. You are not law enforcement. Do not attempt to detain, search, or confront customers. Focus on service responsibilities and safety. When in doubt, step back and involve someone with proper authority to handle the situation.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Workplace policies often include steps for handling safety concerns. These may involve communication protocols, incident reporting, and escalation procedures. Following these policies ensures consistency and protects you from liability. Always rely on your training and management support when facing uncertain situations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Alcohol service in South Carolina carries legal responsibility. If laws are violated, consequences may apply to both the server and the business. These consequences can fall into three main categories: criminal, civil, and administrative. Understanding these categories helps you recognize the risks tied to your decisions and reinforces why following the law matters every shift.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Criminal liability involves breaking the law. This can include selling alcohol to a minor or serving someone who is clearly intoxicated. If charged, a server may face fines or other legal penalties. These consequences are handled through the criminal justice system. Avoiding illegal service decisions helps protect you from criminal charges.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Civil liability involves lawsuits. If alcohol service leads to harm, such as an accident or injury, a business or server may be sued. These cases often involve financial damages. Civil cases are separate from criminal charges. Even if no criminal charge is filed, a civil lawsuit can still occur.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Administrative liability relates to licensing and regulatory action. State agencies can take action against a business permit. This may include fines, suspension, or revocation of the license. These actions directly affect the business’s ability to operate. In South Carolina, certain businesses that sell alcohol for on-premises consumption after 5:00 p.m. must also maintain liquor liability insurance or equivalent coverage as required by law. Violations by staff can lead to consequences for the entire establishment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: All three types of liability can happen from one incident. For example, an underage sale could lead to criminal charges, a lawsuit, and license penalties. This is why every decision matters. Following proper procedures reduces risk and protects both you and your employer from multiple forms of liability.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Servers are expected to follow both state law and workplace policies. Policies are designed to help prevent violations. Ignoring these rules increases risk. If you are unsure about a situation, ask a manager before acting. Taking a moment to verify can prevent serious consequences.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Your actions also affect others. Illegal alcohol service can lead to injuries, accidents, or long-term harm. These outcomes may impact customers, families, and the community. Responsible service is not just about avoiding penalties. It is about protecting people from preventable harm.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Managers and coworkers are resources when handling difficult situations. If a situation feels uncertain or risky, involve management early. Do not try to handle complex issues alone. Team support helps ensure correct decisions and reduces the chance of mistakes that could lead to liability.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.
Click each item to learn more.

sections.title: Unit 2 – Alcohol as a Drug & BAC

contents.text: In this unit we will discuss the following topics:

text.instruction: Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: After a person drinks, alcohol enters the bloodstream. It does not need to be digested like food. Some alcohol is absorbed in the stomach. Most absorption happens in the small intestine. Once in the blood, it travels to the brain. This is why effects can begin within minutes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: The liver processes alcohol over time. It breaks down alcohol at a steady rate. Most people process about one standard drink per hour. Drinking faster than this causes alcohol to build up in the bloodstream. This leads to higher intoxication levels. You cannot speed up this process.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Blood Alcohol Concentration (BAC) measures the amount of alcohol in the blood. As BAC rises, impairment increases. Even small amounts can affect judgment and coordination. Higher BAC levels lead to more serious impairment. Servers should focus on visible signs, not exact BAC numbers.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Not everyone reacts to alcohol the same way. Body size, gender, and health all play a role. Food in the stomach can slow absorption. Medications may increase effects. Experience with alcohol may change how signs appear. Always judge each guest individually.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Alcohol impacts coordination and decision-making. Guests may take risks they normally avoid. Reaction time slows. This increases the chance of injury or unsafe behavior. As a server, you must recognize these changes early. Acting sooner helps prevent problems.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: As intoxication increases, behavior may change. Some guests become louder or more emotional. Others may become quiet or withdrawn. Physical signs often appear with behavior changes. Watch for patterns, not just one sign. Consistent observation is key to safe service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Blood Alcohol Concentration (BAC) measures how much alcohol is in a person’s bloodstream. It is shown as a percentage. For example, 0.08% means alcohol is present in the blood at that level. As BAC increases, impairment increases. Servers do not measure BAC directly, but understanding it helps explain behavior and risk.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: BAC rises when alcohol enters the bloodstream faster than the body can process it. The liver removes alcohol at a steady rate. Drinking multiple beverages quickly causes BAC to increase. This leads to stronger effects and higher impairment. Timing and pace matter as much as total drinks consumed.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: A standard drink is a way to measure alcohol content. Different beverages can contain similar amounts of alcohol. Beer, wine, and liquor vary in size but may have equal alcohol levels. Understanding standard drinks helps you track how much alcohol a guest consumes, even with different beverage types.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Not all drinks are equal in strength. A large pour or strong mix can contain more alcohol than one standard drink. Guests may not realize how much they are consuming. As a server, you should recognize when drinks are stronger than usual. This helps prevent over-service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: BAC does not decrease quickly. Time is the only factor that lowers BAC. Coffee, food, or cold showers do not speed up the process. These may make a person feel more alert, but impairment remains. Never rely on myths when making service decisions.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: As BAC rises, judgment and coordination decline. Guests may take risks or ignore consequences. This increases the chance of harm. Servers must focus on behavior, not just drink count. Recognizing patterns early allows you to slow or stop service before problems occur.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Tracking drinks helps you estimate risk, but it is not exact. Two guests may drink the same amount and show different effects. Always combine drink tracking with observation. This approach improves accuracy and supports compliance with South Carolina service responsibilities.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Servers play a key role in preventing over-service. Understanding BAC and standard drinks helps guide decisions. You are responsible for noticing changes and taking action. Slowing service, offering alternatives, or stopping alcohol service may be necessary to protect the guest and others.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: BAC is affected by more than just how much a person drinks. Individual factors change how alcohol is absorbed and processed. As a server, you must understand these differences. This helps explain why two guests may show different levels of impairment after similar drinking. Always focus on behavior and risk, not assumptions.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Body weight plays a major role in BAC. People with lower body weight usually reach higher BAC levels faster. There is less body mass to absorb the alcohol. Heavier individuals may show effects more slowly, but they can still become impaired. Never assume someone is safe based on size alone.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Food in the stomach slows alcohol absorption. Drinking on an empty stomach allows alcohol to enter the bloodstream faster. This can lead to quicker intoxication. Guests who have not eaten are at higher risk. As a server, you should be aware of how eating patterns affect alcohol impact.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: The rate of drinking is one of the biggest factors affecting BAC. Drinking multiple beverages quickly causes alcohol to build up in the bloodstream. Slower drinking gives the body time to process alcohol. Fast consumption increases the risk of impairment and unsafe behavior.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Medications and drugs can increase alcohol’s effects. Prescription, over-the-counter, and illegal drugs may interact with alcohol. These interactions can cause stronger impairment or unexpected behavior. As a server, you may not know what a guest has taken. Focus on visible signs and changes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Tolerance refers to how a person responds to alcohol over time. Some people appear less affected after drinking. This does not mean alcohol is not impacting them. Their BAC may still be high. Tolerance can hide visible signs, making careful observation even more important.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Environmental factors can also influence alcohol effects. Heat, fatigue, and stress may increase impairment. Loud or crowded settings may make it harder to notice signs. Stay alert and focused on each guest. Your awareness helps prevent over-service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Understanding BAC factors helps you make better service decisions. You cannot measure BAC directly, but you can recognize risk. Combine drink tracking with observation. When in doubt, slow service or stop serving alcohol. Protecting the guest and others is your responsibility.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol elimination is the process of removing alcohol from the body. This happens mainly in the liver. The liver breaks down alcohol at a steady rate. Once alcohol is in the bloodstream, the body must process it over time. This process cannot be rushed or skipped.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: On average, the body eliminates about one standard drink per hour. This rate can vary slightly by person. However, it does not speed up significantly. Drinking faster than this rate causes alcohol to build up in the bloodstream. This leads to increasing impairment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Many people believe certain actions can reduce intoxication quickly. These are common myths. While some actions may make a person feel more alert, they do not lower BAC. Servers must understand the difference between feeling better and being less impaired.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Coffee is a common example. It may increase alertness, but it does not remove alcohol from the body. A guest may seem more awake but still be impaired. This can increase risk because they may believe they are safe when they are not.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Food can slow alcohol absorption if eaten before or during drinking. However, once alcohol is in the bloodstream, food does not remove it. Guests may think eating after drinking will sober them up. This is not accurate.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Fresh air may help a person feel better, but it does not reduce BAC. A guest may appear more stable after stepping outside. However, impairment remains. Always rely on observed signs, not temporary improvements.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: As a server, you must act based on facts, not myths. If a guest shows signs of intoxication, you should slow or stop alcohol service. Do not rely on claims that they have “sobered up.” Your responsibility is to prevent harm.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Understanding elimination helps you make safer decisions. Alcohol takes time to leave the body. Guests cannot speed up this process. Your actions can reduce risk by limiting further alcohol service and offering safer alternatives.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol can interact with many types of drugs. These include prescription, over-the-counter, and illegal substances. When combined, effects can become stronger or unpredictable. As a server, you may not know what a guest has taken. Focus on recognizing unusual or increased impairment and respond appropriately.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Drug interactions can increase intoxication quickly. A person may appear impaired after only one or two drinks. This can happen even if they normally handle alcohol well. These situations require careful observation and early action to prevent harm.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Some prescription medications warn against alcohol use. These include drugs for pain, anxiety, sleep, and mood. Mixing alcohol with these medications can cause stronger sedation or confusion. Guests may not always follow warnings, so you must rely on what you observe.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Over-the-counter medications can also affect alcohol response. Cold medicines and antihistamines may increase drowsiness. Even common medications can change how alcohol affects the body. Do not assume OTC use is harmless when combined with alcohol.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Illegal drugs can create dangerous combinations with alcohol. Effects may include confusion, aggression, or extreme drowsiness. These reactions can appear suddenly. As a server, you must respond quickly if a guest shows unusual or severe impairment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Mixing alcohol with stimulants can be especially risky. A guest may feel alert but still be impaired. This can lead to risky decisions. The masking effect makes it harder to judge intoxication. Focus on behavior and coordination, not energy level.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Drug and alcohol interactions can increase the chance of injury or illness. Guests may lose coordination or awareness. This creates safety risks for them and others. Early intervention helps prevent serious outcomes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: If you suspect drug interaction, slow or stop alcohol service. Offer water or non-alcoholic options. Involve a manager if needed. Stay calm and professional. Your goal is to reduce risk and keep the situation safe.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

sections.title: Unit 3 — Minors & ID Checking
sections.description: South Carolina Alcohol Laws and Liability

contents.text: In this unit we will discuss the following topics:

paragraphs: Preventing underage alcohol sales is a core responsibility in South Carolina. Selling to a minor is illegal and can lead to serious consequences for both the server and the business. Your actions directly impact safety, compliance, and your job security. Every sale decision matters. Following the law protects you, your workplace, and your community from avoidable harm.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Minors face higher risks when alcohol is involved. Their bodies and decision-making skills are still developing. Alcohol use at a young age increases the chance of unsafe behavior and injury. Preventing access reduces these risks. As a server, you play a key role in limiting harm by refusing sales when required and following proper procedures every time.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: South Carolina law requires that alcohol not be sold or served to anyone under 21. Violations can result in penalties for both the server and the establishment. This may include fines or other consequences. Following the law is required. It protects your job and ensures your workplace stays compliant with state requirements.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Checking identification is one of the most effective ways to prevent underage sales. In South Carolina, the safest and most consistent practice is to check identification for every person before selling or serving alcohol. Never rely on appearance alone. Consistent ID checks reduce mistakes. Even one failure can result in a violation. Make ID verification a routine part of your service process.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Underage drinking can have long-term effects. It can impact education, judgment, and future opportunities. Early alcohol use is linked to increased risk-taking behavior. In addition to legal consequences, some alcohol-related offenses may affect educational opportunities and, in some cases, eligibility for certain state scholarships. South Carolina also offers prevention and recovery resources such as MYDORWAY for young people and families dealing with substance use concerns. By refusing a sale, you may be preventing serious harm. Your role goes beyond completing a transaction. You are helping protect someone’s future.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Your responsibility includes making decisions that follow the law and company policies. Refusing a sale may feel uncomfortable, but it is necessary. Staying calm and confident helps you handle these situations correctly. Doing the right thing protects you, your employer, and others in the environment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Minors may attempt to buy alcohol using fake or borrowed identification. Stay alert and follow proper ID-checking steps. If something does not seem right, take time to verify. Do not rush. Careful checking helps prevent illegal sales and protects you from making a costly mistake.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Managers are a resource when situations become difficult. If a customer becomes pushy or confrontational, ask for help. You are not expected to handle every situation alone. Team support helps ensure rules are followed consistently and safely across the business.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Consistency is key to preventing underage sales. Apply the same process to every customer. Do not make exceptions based on pressure or assumptions. Following the same steps each time reduces risk. Consistent actions help protect you and ensure compliance with South Carolina alcohol laws.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: In South Carolina, alcohol servers are legally responsible for following state laws when selling or serving alcohol. This includes refusing sales to minors and not serving intoxicated individuals. You are accountable for your actions during every shift. Failing to follow the law can result in penalties for you and your employer. Understanding your responsibility helps you make better decisions in real situations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Selling alcohol to a person under 21 is illegal in South Carolina. This applies even if the customer appears older or presents questionable identification. You are expected to verify age before completing a sale. If you fail to do so, you may face legal consequences. Following proper ID procedures helps you avoid violations and stay compliant with the law.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Serving alcohol to an intoxicated person is also prohibited. You must recognize signs of impairment and stop service when necessary. Continuing to serve someone who is clearly intoxicated increases risk. It can lead to harm, including accidents or injuries. Your role includes monitoring customers and making decisions that protect safety.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Legal responsibility applies to both individual servers and the business. Even if a manager is present, you are still accountable for your actions. You cannot ignore the law because of pressure or instructions from others. Each server must follow legal requirements during every transaction. Personal accountability is part of responsible alcohol service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Criminal penalties may apply if laws are broken. These penalties depend on the situation and the violation. Selling to a minor or serving an intoxicated person can result in charges. Legal consequences can affect your record and future employment. Following the law helps you avoid these outcomes and protects your professional reputation.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Administrative penalties may impact the business. This can include fines or actions taken against the establishment’s license. These penalties can affect all employees. Even one violation can cause problems for the entire operation. Your actions contribute to the overall compliance of the business.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Civil liability may occur if someone is harmed after being served alcohol illegally. This could involve injuries or damages linked to alcohol service. While you may not see immediate effects, your actions can contribute to serious outcomes. Preventing illegal service helps reduce the risk of harm and legal claims.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Law enforcement and regulatory agencies monitor alcohol sales. This may include compliance checks or investigations. You are expected to follow the law at all times, not just when being observed. Consistent compliance helps you avoid violations and ensures your workplace meets state requirements.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Making the correct decision in the moment is critical. If you are unsure about a situation, stop and verify. Ask for identification or involve a manager. Do not guess or take shortcuts. Taking a few extra seconds to follow proper steps can prevent serious legal consequences.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: In South Carolina, the safest and most consistent practice is to check identification for every person before selling or serving alcohol. This rule helps prevent illegal sales and protects you from violations. You cannot rely on guesswork, assumptions, or appearance. Consistent ID checks are a key part of responsible alcohol service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: A customer’s appearance is not a reliable way to judge legal age. Some customers may look older than they are, and others may look younger than their actual age. Because of this, you must apply a consistent approach with every customer. In South Carolina, the safest and most consistent practice is to check identification for every person before completing a sale.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Failing to check ID is one of the most common reasons for underage sales. Even if a customer is actually 21, you can still face consequences if you did not verify their age. The law requires proper ID checking, not assumptions. Taking the extra step protects you and your workplace.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Consistency is critical. You must apply the same ID-checking process to every customer. Do not make exceptions for regulars, coworkers, or customers who seem trustworthy. Treat every situation the same. This reduces risk and helps you stay compliant during every shift.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Busy environments can create pressure to move quickly. Long lines or impatient customers may tempt you to skip steps. Do not let this affect your decisions. Taking a few seconds to check ID is always worth it. Speed should never replace proper procedure when alcohol is involved.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Checking ID also protects minors from harm. Preventing access to alcohol reduces the risk of unsafe behavior and poor decisions. Your role is not just about following rules. You are helping create a safer environment by stopping illegal sales before they happen.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: If a customer refuses to show ID, you must refuse the sale. There are no exceptions to this rule. A valid ID is required to verify age. Do not accept verbal confirmation or pressure from the customer. Following this rule protects you from making an illegal sale.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Managers and coworkers can support you when enforcing ID rules. If a situation becomes difficult, ask for help. You are not expected to handle confrontational situations alone. Team support helps ensure rules are followed consistently and safely across the workplace.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Building the habit of always checking ID makes your job easier. When it becomes routine, you are less likely to make mistakes. Strong habits reduce risk and improve consistency. Over time, this approach helps protect you, your employer, and your customers.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: In South Carolina, you must verify a customer’s age using valid identification before selling or serving alcohol. Not all IDs are acceptable. You are responsible for knowing which forms are valid. Accepting the wrong ID can lead to an illegal sale. Always take time to review identification carefully before completing a transaction.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Valid identification must be government-issued and include key details. This includes a photo, date of birth, and identifying information. The ID must allow you to confirm the customer is at least 21 years old. If any required element is missing or unclear, you should not accept the ID.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Common valid forms of identification include a driver’s license issued by a U.S. state. These IDs are widely used and contain the required information. Reviewing real examples of South Carolina IDs helps you recognize the layout, the location of the date of birth, and the overall appearance of the document. You should still examine each ID closely. Do not assume an ID is valid just because it looks familiar. Always verify the details before completing a sale.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: State-issued identification cards are also acceptable. These are issued to individuals who do not drive but still need official identification. They include similar features to a driver’s license. As with all IDs, check the photo, date of birth, and expiration date before accepting it.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Military identification may also be presented. These IDs are issued by the U.S. government and include identifying information. Review the ID carefully to confirm it belongs to the person presenting it. If you are unsure how to verify it, ask a manager for assistance before proceeding.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Expired identification is not valid for alcohol sales. Even if the date of birth shows the customer is over 21, an expired ID should not be accepted. Expiration dates must be current. Always check this detail as part of your standard ID verification process.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: You must compare the photo on the ID to the person presenting it. Look at facial features, not just hair or clothing. If the photo does not match, do not accept the ID. This step helps prevent the use of borrowed identification and reduces the risk of illegal sales.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Always check the date of birth carefully. Confirm that the customer is at least 21 years old on the day of the sale. Take your time and do the math if needed. Mistakes in age calculation can lead to violations. Accuracy is essential when verifying eligibility.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: If you are unsure about an ID, do not guess. Ask for help from a manager or follow company procedures. It is better to delay a sale than to make a mistake. Taking extra time ensures you are following the law and protects you from potential penalties.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Checking identification is a step-by-step process that must be followed every time. Skipping steps increases the risk of an illegal sale. A consistent routine helps you stay accurate and confident. In South Carolina, proper ID checking should be part of every alcohol sale or service interaction. Building a clear process helps you avoid mistakes and stay compliant.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Start by asking for identification before completing the sale. Do this early in the interaction to avoid confusion later. Be polite but direct. Customers should expect this step. Asking clearly and confidently sets the tone and helps prevent pushback. This first step is critical to a smooth and compliant process.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Once you receive the ID, hold it in your hand. Do not try to verify it from a distance. Holding the ID allows you to examine details more closely. Take your time. A quick glance is not enough. Careful inspection helps you catch issues that could lead to an illegal sale.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Check the overall condition of the ID. Look for signs of damage, wear, or tampering. If the ID appears altered or unusual, do not accept it. Even small issues can indicate a problem. If something does not look right, trust your judgment and follow your procedures.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Verify the photo by comparing the face on the ID to the person in front of you. Focus on facial features, not clothing or hairstyle. If the photo does not clearly match the person, you should not proceed with the sale. This step helps prevent the use of borrowed IDs.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Check the date of birth carefully. Confirm the customer is at least 21 years old. Take a moment to calculate if needed. Do not rush this step. Mistakes here can lead to serious consequences. Accuracy is more important than speed during ID verification.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Look at the expiration date next. An expired ID is not valid for alcohol sales. Even if the customer is over 21, you must refuse the sale if the ID is expired. Always include this step in your process to avoid accepting invalid identification.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Check for any signs of alteration. This may include mismatched fonts, peeling surfaces, or unclear printing. These signs can indicate a fake or tampered ID. If you notice anything unusual, do not accept the ID. Follow your procedures and involve a manager if needed.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Take your time during the entire process. Rushing increases the chance of mistakes. Even in busy situations, slow down enough to complete each step correctly. A few extra seconds can prevent serious legal consequences. Consistency and attention to detail are key to proper ID checking.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Following a step-by-step process makes ID checking easier and more reliable. When you use the same routine every time, you reduce risk and improve accuracy. This protects you, your employer, and your customers. Building strong habits ensures you stay compliant during every shift.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: After checking the basic details on an ID, continue the process by confirming it is genuine. A valid ID must be more than just correct information. It must also be authentic. This step helps prevent the use of fake or altered identification. Taking time here protects you from making an illegal sale.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Look closely at the physical features of the ID. Pay attention to texture, thickness, and overall quality. Government-issued IDs are consistent in appearance. If something feels off, do not ignore it. Trust your instincts and take extra time to review the ID carefully before continuing.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Check the information across the ID for consistency. Names, dates, and other details should align correctly. If something does not match or seems unusual, do not accept the ID. Inconsistencies are a strong warning sign that the ID may not be valid.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Ask simple questions if needed. For example, you may ask the customer to confirm their birthdate or address. A person using a fake ID may hesitate or give incorrect answers. This step can help you confirm whether the ID belongs to the person presenting it.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Watch the customer’s behavior during the process. Nervousness, rushing, or avoiding eye contact may indicate a problem. While behavior alone is not proof, it can support your decision to take a closer look. Stay calm and focused as you complete your checks.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: If the ID passes all checks, you may proceed with the sale. Only complete the transaction after you are confident the ID is valid and the customer is at least 21. Never rush this decision. Confidence in your verification process is essential.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Do not return to the sale once you have decided to refuse it. Changing your decision can lead to mistakes. Stay firm and consistent. If needed, step away and ask for assistance. Your safety and compliance are the priority in every situation.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Accurately verifying age is required before selling alcohol in South Carolina. You must confirm that a customer is at least 21 years old. This is done by checking the date of birth on a valid ID. Guessing or estimating is not acceptable. Taking time to verify age protects you from making an illegal sale.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Age verification starts by locating the date of birth on the ID. This is usually clearly labeled, but it may not appear in the same place on every ID. On South Carolina IDs, you should become familiar with where the date of birth appears so you can find it quickly without confusing it with other dates on the card. Take a moment to find it and read it carefully. Misreading numbers can lead to mistakes. Always confirm you are looking at the correct date before making a decision.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: A simple way to verify age is to subtract 21 years from today’s date. This gives you the latest acceptable birthdate for legal sales. If the customer was born on or before that date, they are eligible. If their birthdate is after that date, they are underage and you must refuse the sale.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Pay close attention to both the year and the exact date. A customer may have turned 21 recently or may still be underage by a few days. You must confirm that they have already reached their 21st birthday. Being close is not enough. They must be fully 21 on the day of the sale.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Do not rush the calculation. Take a moment to confirm the numbers. Mistakes often happen when servers move too quickly. Even during busy times, accuracy is critical. A small error in reading or calculating can lead to a serious violation.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Some IDs may format the date differently. For example, the order of month, day, and year may vary. Always take time to understand the format before making a decision. Misreading the format can lead to incorrect age verification.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: If you are unsure about the age calculation, stop and double-check. You can also ask a manager for assistance. It is better to pause than to make a mistake. Taking extra time helps ensure you are following the law and protects you from penalties.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Consistency is key when verifying age. Follow the same steps for every customer. This reduces the chance of errors and keeps your process reliable. Strong habits improve accuracy and help you stay compliant during every shift.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Remember, you are responsible for the final decision. Even if a customer insists they are old enough, you must verify it yourself. Do not rely on statements or pressure. Only complete the sale when you are confident the customer is legally 21 or older.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Fake identification is commonly used by minors attempting to buy alcohol. As a server in South Carolina, you are responsible for identifying and refusing fake IDs. Accepting one can lead to an illegal sale and serious consequences. Learning how to spot red flags helps you make better decisions and stay compliant with the law.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: A fake ID may look real at first glance. That is why you must slow down and inspect it carefully. Do not rely on a quick check. Take time to examine details and confirm authenticity. Careful inspection helps you detect problems that could lead to violations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Look closely at the photo on the ID. Compare it to the person presenting it. Pay attention to facial features, not just hair or clothing. If the photo does not clearly match the customer, do not accept the ID. This is a common sign of borrowed or fake identification.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Check the information for consistency. Names, dates, and other details should align correctly. If something does not match or seems unusual, it may be a fake ID. Even small inconsistencies can indicate a problem. Always take time to review all details carefully.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Examine the physical condition of the ID. Government-issued IDs are made with consistent materials and quality. If the ID feels too thin, too thick, or damaged, it may not be valid. Trust your observations and follow your procedures if something seems off.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Look for signs of tampering. This may include scratched surfaces, lifted corners, or altered numbers. These changes are often used to modify birthdates. If you notice any signs of alteration, you must refuse the sale. Never ignore these warning signs.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: You can ask simple questions to help confirm identity. Ask the customer to state their birthdate or address. If their answers do not match the ID, this is a strong warning sign. Use this step when you are unsure, but always rely on the ID itself as your main source.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: If you believe an ID is fake, do not accept it. Refuse the sale and follow your workplace procedures. Stay calm and professional. Do not argue with the customer. Your goal is to enforce the law while maintaining a safe environment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: If you are unsure, ask a manager for assistance. It is better to pause than to make a mistake. Team support helps ensure correct decisions. Protecting yourself and your workplace should always come first when handling questionable identification.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: When you suspect an ID may be fake, your role is to slow down and follow proper procedures. Do not ignore warning signs. Accepting a fake ID can lead to an illegal sale and serious consequences. You are responsible for verifying identity and age before completing any alcohol transaction in South Carolina.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: If something feels off about an ID, trust your judgment. You are not required to prove it is fake. You only need to decide whether you can confidently verify it as valid. If you cannot confirm it, you must not proceed with the sale. This protects you from making a mistake.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Ask the customer simple questions if needed. For example, ask them to confirm their birthdate or address. A person using a fake ID may hesitate or give incorrect answers. This can help support your decision, but the ID itself must still be your primary source for verification.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Watch how the customer behaves. Nervous actions, rushing you, or avoiding eye contact may indicate a problem. These behaviors alone do not prove the ID is fake, but they can support your decision to take a closer look. Stay calm and focused during the interaction.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: If you cannot confirm the ID is valid, you must refuse the sale. Do not take risks. The law requires you to verify age with valid identification. If you are unsure, that means you cannot verify it. Refusing the sale is the correct and safe decision.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Stay professional when refusing a sale. Use a calm tone and clear language. Avoid arguing or accusing the customer. You can simply state that you are unable to accept the ID. Keeping the interaction respectful helps prevent escalation and keeps the situation under control.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Do not return to the sale once you have refused it. Changing your decision increases risk and can lead to mistakes. Stay firm. If the customer continues to push, repeat your decision and involve management if necessary. Consistency is key to maintaining compliance.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Never accept pressure from customers or coworkers to override your decision. You are responsible for the sale. Even in busy situations, you must follow proper procedures. Protecting yourself and your workplace is more important than completing a transaction.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: If a situation becomes difficult or confrontational, step away and ask for help. Managers are there to support you. Your safety matters. Do not engage in arguments. Following escalation procedures helps keep everyone safe and ensures rules are enforced properly.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Refusing underage sales is a required part of your role in South Carolina. You must not sell alcohol to anyone under 21 or anyone who cannot provide valid ID. This decision protects you, your employer, and the public. Even if it feels uncomfortable, refusing the sale is the correct action. Confidence in this step helps prevent mistakes and supports compliance.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Refusing a sale should always be done clearly and professionally. Use simple, direct language and avoid long explanations. You do not need to argue or justify the law. State your decision and stay consistent. Keeping your response short and firm helps reduce confusion and prevents the situation from escalating further.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Customers may react with frustration when refused. This is common and should be expected. Stay calm and do not take it personally. Your role is to enforce the law, not to win an argument. Remaining composed helps you manage the situation safely and professionally.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Avoid arguing with the customer. Arguing can make the situation worse and increase risk. Instead, repeat your decision if needed and keep your tone steady. Do not engage in back-and-forth discussion. Staying consistent helps end the interaction more quickly and safely.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: If a customer becomes more upset, create space and avoid escalation. Do not raise your voice or respond emotionally. Focus on staying professional and following your procedures. Keeping distance when needed helps maintain safety for you and others in the area.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Involve a manager when a situation becomes difficult. Managers are there to support you and handle escalations. Asking for help shows good judgment. It also helps ensure the situation is handled correctly and consistently with company policies.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Never change your decision after refusing a sale. Once you determine the customer is underage or cannot provide valid ID, the sale must not happen. Going back on your decision can lead to mistakes and legal consequences. Stay firm and consistent every time.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Do not let pressure from customers or coworkers influence your decision. You are responsible for the sale. Even during busy shifts, you must follow the law. Taking shortcuts can lead to serious consequences. Staying consistent protects you and your workplace from violations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Refusing underage sales supports a safer environment. It helps prevent harm and reduces risk in your community. Your actions make a difference in real situations. Taking your role seriously helps protect others and reinforces responsible alcohol service standards.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

sections.title: Unit 4 — Preventing Sales & Service to Minors

contents.text: In this unit we will discuss the following topics:

paragraphs: Intoxication means a person has consumed enough alcohol to impair normal mental or physical abilities. This affects judgment, coordination, and reaction time. In South Carolina, you must not serve someone who is overly intoxicated. Your responsibility is to recognize impairment early and act before it creates risk for the guest, your team, or the public.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol is a depressant that slows the central nervous system. As drinking increases, brain function becomes less controlled. Decision-making declines, and reaction time slows. Physical coordination also weakens. Even small increases in alcohol can change behavior. Understanding these effects helps you identify intoxication before it becomes obvious or dangerous.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: The early stage of impairment often includes mood changes and reduced inhibitions. Guests may become more talkative or confident. They may drink faster or encourage others to drink. While this may seem harmless, it signals the start of impaired judgment. This is the best time to slow service and monitor behavior closely.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: As intoxication increases, coordination and focus begin to decline. Guests may struggle to follow conversations or track time. They may forget what they ordered or repeat themselves. These signs show alcohol is affecting brain function more significantly. At this point, continued service increases risk and may lead to illegal over-service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: In later stages, intoxication becomes obvious and serious. Guests may stumble, drop items, or have trouble standing. Speech may be difficult to understand. Emotional changes like aggression or sadness may appear. At this point, the guest should not be served alcohol under any circumstances. Immediate action is required to manage the situation safely.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Severe intoxication can lead to dangerous health risks. A guest may become unresponsive or unable to stay awake. Breathing may slow, and vomiting may occur. These are medical warning signs. Alcohol poisoning is possible. This situation requires immediate attention, including involving management and seeking medical help if needed.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Not all guests show intoxication the same way. Some may appear calm while still being impaired. Others may become loud or emotional. You must rely on observable signs, not assumptions. Consistent monitoring is key. Always base your decisions on behavior, not how much a guest claims they have consumed.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Physical signs of intoxication are visible changes in how a person moves, speaks, or appears. These signs help you identify impairment without relying on what a guest says. In South Carolina, recognizing these signs is critical because you must not serve someone who is overly intoxicated. Your decision must be based on what you observe, not assumptions.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: As alcohol affects the body, coordination begins to decline. Guests may have trouble with simple movements like picking up a drink or standing up. Small physical mistakes are often early indicators. Paying attention to these changes allows you to adjust service before impairment becomes severe or unsafe.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Balance and coordination continue to worsen as intoxication increases. A guest may bump into objects or need support while walking. They may misjudge distances or struggle to sit down properly. These physical signs show the body is losing control, which increases the risk of injury and unsafe behavior.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Hand-eye coordination is often affected during intoxication. Guests may spill drinks, miss objects, or fumble with money or cards. These mistakes may seem minor but are important indicators. They show that alcohol is impairing basic motor skills and that continued service could make the situation worse.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Speech patterns can reveal intoxication. Words may become slurred, sentences may trail off, or the guest may repeat themselves. Speech changes are a reliable sign that alcohol is affecting brain function. These signs should not be ignored when deciding whether to continue service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Facial appearance can also change with intoxication. A guest may have flushed skin, drooping eyelids, or a blank expression. Eyes may appear unfocused or watery. These physical changes provide additional evidence of impairment and should be considered along with other observed behaviors.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Not every guest will show the same physical signs. Some may appear steady while still being impaired. Others may show obvious symptoms quickly. You must look at the full picture, including multiple signs. Consistency in observation helps you make better decisions and avoid illegal service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Behavioral signs of intoxication are changes in how a person acts, speaks, or interacts with others. These signs often appear before severe physical symptoms. In South Carolina, recognizing these behaviors is critical because you must not serve alcohol to an overly intoxicated person. Your decisions should be based on observable behavior, not assumptions or what the guest says.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol affects judgment early. Guests may begin making poor decisions, such as ordering drinks too quickly or ignoring limits. They may act more confident than usual or take risks they normally would not. These early behavioral changes are important warning signs that intoxication is developing.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: As intoxication increases, behavior becomes less controlled. Guests may have trouble focusing on conversations or following directions. They may forget what they ordered or repeat themselves. These signs indicate that alcohol is affecting brain function and decision-making more significantly.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Emotional changes are common with intoxication. A guest may become unusually happy, angry, or sad without a clear reason. Mood swings can happen quickly. These changes can lead to unpredictable behavior, which increases the risk of conflict or unsafe situations in your establishment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Intoxicated guests may also lose awareness of their surroundings. They may invade personal space, speak loudly without noticing, or ignore social cues. This behavior can make other guests uncomfortable and create tension. Recognizing these signs helps you step in before problems escalate.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Decision-making becomes unreliable as intoxication progresses. A guest may insist they are fine when they are not. They may try to order more alcohol even after showing clear signs of impairment. You must rely on your observations, not the guest’s opinion, when deciding to continue service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Not all intoxicated guests act the same way. Some may become quiet and withdrawn, while others become loud and disruptive. Both can be impaired. You must observe behavior patterns over time and look for multiple signs before making a decision about service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Environmental and individual risk factors affect how quickly a person becomes intoxicated. These factors can increase or decrease impairment, even if two people drink the same amount. In South Carolina, you must recognize these risks to prevent over-service. Your role is to adjust service based on conditions, not just drink count.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Individual tolerance varies from person to person. Some guests may show signs of intoxication after fewer drinks, while others may appear less affected. Tolerance does not prevent impairment. A guest with high tolerance may still be unsafe. Always rely on observed behavior, not assumptions about drinking experience.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Food intake plays a major role in alcohol absorption. Guests who drink on an empty stomach may become intoxicated faster. Food slows the absorption of alcohol into the bloodstream. If a guest has not eaten, you should be more cautious and monitor for early signs of impairment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Medications and drugs can increase the effects of alcohol. Prescription, over-the-counter, and illegal substances may interact with alcohol in unpredictable ways. This can lead to faster or stronger impairment. You may not always know what a guest has taken, so watch closely for unusual or rapid changes in behavior.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Environmental factors can influence drinking behavior. Loud music, crowded spaces, and group settings may encourage faster drinking. Guests may feel pressured to keep up with others. These conditions can lead to quicker intoxication, even if the guest does not intend to drink heavily.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Time is another important factor. Alcohol takes time to affect the body. A guest may not appear intoxicated immediately after drinking. Effects can build over time. This delay can lead to over-service if you are not careful. Monitor guests consistently, even if they seem fine at first.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Fatigue and stress can also increase the effects of alcohol. A tired or stressed guest may become impaired more quickly. These factors are not always visible, but they can affect behavior. Pay attention to signs of rapid change, even if the guest has not consumed many drinks.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Preventing over-service means taking action before a guest becomes overly intoxicated. In South Carolina, you must not serve alcohol to someone who is already intoxicated. The best approach is to prevent that situation from happening. This requires active monitoring, good communication, and consistent decision-making throughout the guest’s visit.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Start monitoring guests from their first drink. Do not wait until signs of intoxication appear. Track how quickly drinks are ordered and consumed. Pay attention to behavior changes over time. Early awareness allows you to make small adjustments that prevent larger problems later in the shift.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Serving one drink at a time helps you stay in control. Avoid delivering multiple alcoholic drinks to one guest at once. This reduces the chance of rapid consumption. It also gives you time to observe behavior between drinks and make better decisions about continued service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Offer food whenever possible. Food slows alcohol absorption and reduces the speed of intoxication. Suggest appetizers or meals, especially if a guest is drinking quickly. This is a simple and effective way to support safer consumption without directly limiting the guest.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Communication with your team is essential. Share concerns about a guest with coworkers and managers. This ensures consistent service decisions. If one staff member stops service, others should not continue serving that guest. Team awareness prevents mixed messages and unsafe outcomes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Watch for changes over time, not just single actions. A guest may seem fine at one moment but show signs later. Track patterns such as increasing speed of drinking or changing behavior. Consistent observation helps you act at the right time and avoid over-service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Avoid pressure situations. Guests may try to order rounds, participate in drinking games, or encourage others to drink more. These situations increase risk. Stay focused on safe service. Do not allow group behavior to override your responsibility to prevent intoxication.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Recognizing problem drinking means identifying patterns that go beyond normal social use. This includes repeated excessive drinking, loss of control, or behavior that creates risk. In South Carolina, you must act when a guest becomes overly intoxicated. Early recognition helps you intervene before the situation becomes unsafe or illegal.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Problem drinking is often shown through patterns, not just one moment. A guest may consistently drink too quickly or return frequently in an impaired state. These patterns increase the risk of over-service. Watching for repeated behavior helps you identify when stronger action may be needed.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Guests showing problem drinking behaviors may try to hide their level of intoxication. They may insist they are fine or try to order from different staff members. This makes teamwork critical. Always communicate with coworkers to ensure consistent service decisions and prevent continued alcohol service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Emotional or behavioral instability can also signal a problem. A guest may become aggressive, overly emotional, or unpredictable. These behaviors increase safety risks for staff and other guests. Recognizing these changes helps you decide when to step in and limit or stop service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Know when to act. You should take action as soon as you observe clear signs of intoxication or risky behavior. Waiting too long can lead to unsafe conditions. Acting early allows you to manage the situation calmly and reduces the chance of conflict.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Intervention does not always mean immediate refusal. It may begin with slowing service, offering food or water, or checking in with the guest. These steps can help prevent further intoxication. If behavior continues or worsens, stronger action will be required.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Do not rely on what the guest tells you. A guest may say they have only had one drink or that they feel fine. Your responsibility is to observe behavior and make decisions based on what you see. This protects you and your establishment from liability.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

contents.text: In this unit we will discuss the following topics:

paragraphs: Refusing service is a legal and professional responsibility in South Carolina. You must not serve alcohol to someone who is underage or already intoxicated. This is not optional. Failing to refuse service can lead to penalties for both you and your employer. Your decisions directly impact safety, compliance, and your job security.
