text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: You must refuse service if a customer is under 21. This includes any situation where valid identification is not provided. If you cannot confirm age, you cannot complete the sale. Guessing or assuming age is never acceptable. Always rely on proper ID verification before serving alcohol.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: South Carolina law prohibits selling alcohol to an intoxicated person. This applies even if the customer was served elsewhere first. If they are visibly impaired, you must refuse service. It does not matter how they became intoxicated. Your responsibility begins the moment you interact with them.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Legal justification for refusal is based on protecting public safety. Serving an intoxicated person increases the risk of accidents, injuries, and illegal behavior. Refusing service helps prevent harm and keeps your business compliant with state law. It also protects you from personal liability.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: You are also required to refuse service if a customer becomes disruptive or unsafe. This includes threatening behavior, harassment, or refusal to follow rules. Alcohol service should never continue in a situation that risks harm to staff or other customers.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Refusal should be firm, calm, and respectful. Do not argue or debate with the customer. Clearly state that you cannot serve them and avoid blaming language. Keep your tone professional and focused on policy and safety, not personal judgment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Do not offer alternatives that involve alcohol. Instead, suggest non-alcoholic options or food if appropriate. In some cases, offering water or helping arrange transportation may be appropriate. These actions support safety without violating service laws.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Always follow your establishment’s policies when refusing service. This may include documenting the incident or notifying a supervisor. Consistent enforcement helps protect the business and shows compliance with training and legal expectations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Refusing service should follow a clear, consistent process. This helps you stay professional and reduces conflict. A step-by-step approach keeps your actions aligned with the law and your workplace policies. It also shows confidence and control in difficult situations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 1: Observe the customer carefully. Look for signs of intoxication or behavior that requires refusal. Do not rush the decision. Take a moment to confirm what you are seeing before acting. Accurate observation is the foundation of a proper refusal.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Step 2: Decide clearly and confidently. Once you determine that service should stop, commit to that decision. Do not hesitate or second-guess yourself. Waiting too long can make the situation worse and increase risk for everyone involved.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 3: Choose the right moment. Refuse service before pouring or delivering another drink. Timing matters. Acting early prevents escalation and keeps the interaction more manageable. Do not wait until the customer becomes more impaired.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 4: Stay calm and professional. Your tone and body language should remain controlled. Avoid showing frustration or anger. A calm approach reduces tension and makes it easier to communicate your decision clearly.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Step 5: Be direct and respectful. Clearly tell the customer you cannot serve them more alcohol. Do not argue or debate. Keep your explanation short and based on policy or safety. Respectful communication helps prevent escalation.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 6: Do not negotiate. Once you refuse service, the decision is final. Do not offer “just one more” or make exceptions. Changing your decision weakens your authority and creates confusion for the customer.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 7: Stay aware of your surroundings. Watch how the customer reacts. Be ready to involve management or coworkers if needed. Your safety and the safety of others should always come first.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: This section continues the refusal process after you have told the customer you cannot serve more alcohol. What you do next is just as important as the refusal itself. Proper follow-through helps prevent escalation, protects safety, and ensures compliance with South Carolina expectations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 8: Do not continue alcohol service under any circumstance. Once you refuse, the decision stands. Do not serve the customer again during that visit. Consistency is critical. Changing your decision increases risk and weakens enforcement.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Step 9: Offer non-alcoholic alternatives when appropriate. You may suggest water or food. This can help de-escalate the situation and support customer safety. Do not suggest anything that includes alcohol.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 10: Monitor the customer’s behavior. Continue observing after refusal. Watch for signs of escalation, aggression, or worsening impairment. Staying aware helps you respond quickly if the situation changes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 11: Involve management when needed. If the customer becomes upset, refuses to comply, or creates a disturbance, notify a manager immediately. Managers are responsible for supporting staff and handling difficult situations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Step 12: Keep a safe distance. Do not crowd the customer or invade their space. Position yourself where you can exit if needed. Your safety always comes first. Avoid physical contact unless absolutely necessary and directed by policy.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 13: Follow house procedures for documentation. Some workplaces require incident reports when service is refused. Accurate documentation helps protect the business and shows that proper steps were taken.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Step 14: Support safe outcomes. If appropriate, help arrange a safe ride or encourage the customer to avoid driving. While not required in every situation, promoting safety aligns with responsible service practices.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Communication is critical when refusing service. The way you speak can either calm a situation or make it worse. Your goal is to stay in control, reduce tension, and keep everyone safe. Clear, respectful communication helps you enforce the law without escalating conflict.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Always keep your tone calm and steady. Avoid raising your voice or showing frustration. Customers may already be impaired or emotional. A calm tone helps prevent the situation from escalating and shows professionalism.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Use clear and direct language. Avoid long explanations or complicated reasoning. Keep your message short and focused. This reduces confusion and limits opportunities for argument.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Avoid blaming the customer. Do not say things like “you are drunk.” Instead, focus on policy and safety. This keeps the interaction less personal and more professional.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Use “I” statements when refusing service. This keeps the message controlled and less confrontational. It also shows that you are following rules, not making a personal decision.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Listen without arguing. Let the customer speak if they respond, but do not engage in debate. Acknowledge what they say without agreeing to continue service. This helps reduce tension while maintaining your decision.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Do not match the customer’s emotions. If they become loud or upset, stay calm. Matching their tone can escalate the situation. Staying controlled helps bring the situation down, not up.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Use body language that supports de-escalation. Stand at a safe distance, keep your hands visible, and avoid aggressive gestures. Nonverbal communication can affect how your message is received.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Difficult or confrontational customers are a real part of alcohol service. These situations often happen after refusal. Your goal is to protect yourself, others, and the business. You are not expected to win an argument. You are expected to maintain safety and follow the law.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Stay calm and do not take behavior personally. Intoxicated customers may act irrationally. Their reactions are not about you. Keeping this mindset helps you stay focused and avoid emotional responses that can escalate the situation.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Do not argue or try to prove a point. Arguments increase tension and rarely solve the problem. Your role is not to convince the customer they are wrong. Your role is to enforce the decision and keep the environment safe.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Set clear boundaries. Politely but firmly repeat your refusal if needed. Keep your message consistent. Changing your wording or tone too much can create confusion and invite pushback.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Avoid physical contact. Do not touch or attempt to restrain a customer unless directed by policy and trained to do so. Physical interaction can quickly escalate the situation and create safety risks.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Involve management early when a customer becomes confrontational. Do not wait for the situation to escalate further. Managers are responsible for handling higher-risk situations and supporting staff decisions.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: If a customer refuses to leave or becomes disruptive, follow your establishment’s procedures. This may include contacting management or security. Do not try to handle serious situations alone.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Keep other customers in mind. Confrontations can affect the entire environment. Stay professional and avoid creating a scene. Your actions help maintain order and protect the experience of others.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Escalation procedures are used when a situation goes beyond a normal refusal. This includes customers who become aggressive, refuse to follow instructions, or create safety concerns for others. Knowing when to escalate is part of your responsibility. Acting early can prevent injury, property damage, or legal issues for you and your business.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: You should involve management anytime you feel unsure, pressured, or unsafe. Do not try to handle a serious situation alone. Managers are responsible for supporting staff and making higher-level decisions. Bringing them in early helps maintain control and shows that proper procedures are being followed.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Use clear and simple communication when requesting help. State what is happening, where you are, and what support you need. Avoid long explanations or emotional language. This helps management understand the situation quickly and respond without confusion or delay.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Stay aware of the customer while waiting for help. Do not turn your back or lose track of their behavior. Situations can change quickly, especially with intoxicated individuals. Staying alert allows you to respond if the situation escalates further before support arrives.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Follow your establishment’s specific escalation procedures at all times. These may include notifying a supervisor, calling security, or alerting other staff members. Knowing these steps ahead of time ensures a faster and more organized response during stressful situations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Once management arrives, allow them to take the lead. Do not interrupt or attempt to override their decisions. Step back but stay available if they need information. This keeps communication clear and ensures the situation is handled in a coordinated way.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Maintain a safe distance from the customer at all times. Position yourself where you have a clear path to exit if needed. Avoid getting trapped in tight spaces. Your safety is always the priority, especially in unpredictable situations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Avoid physical involvement unless you are trained and directed to act. Trying to physically manage a customer can increase risk and lead to injury. Let trained personnel or management handle any situation that may require physical intervention.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Offering alternatives is an important part of responsible alcohol service. When you refuse alcohol, you should still focus on safety and customer care. Alternatives can help reduce tension and prevent harm. These actions support your legal responsibility to avoid over-service and protect the public.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alternatives do not replace refusal. Once you stop alcohol service, that decision must stand. Alternatives are meant to support the customer after refusal, not change the outcome. This helps keep your actions consistent with South Carolina expectations and your workplace policies.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Offering water is a simple and effective step. It helps the customer stay hydrated and may slow further impairment. Providing water also shows care and professionalism. While it does not reduce intoxication immediately, it supports safer outcomes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Food can be offered if appropriate. While food does not quickly reverse intoxication, it may help slow alcohol absorption if consumed earlier. Offering food can also give the customer something to focus on and may help calm the situation.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Non-alcoholic beverages are another good option. Suggesting soda, juice, or other alcohol-free drinks allows the customer to stay engaged without further alcohol consumption. This can help reduce pressure and maintain a positive interaction.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Transportation is one of the most important alternatives. If a customer is impaired, they should not drive. Encouraging safe transportation helps prevent accidents and protects both the customer and the public.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: You may suggest calling a ride service, contacting a friend, or arranging another safe way home. While you may not be required to arrange transportation yourself, encouraging safe choices is part of responsible service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Do not force alternatives on the customer. Offer them calmly and respectfully. If the customer refuses, continue to focus on safety and follow your workplace procedures. Your role is to guide, not control, their decisions beyond service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Protecting yourself legally is a key part of alcohol service in South Carolina. Your actions can have legal consequences if laws are not followed. Refusing service when required helps reduce your risk. Following proper procedures shows that you acted responsibly. Documentation can also support your decisions if questions come up later.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: You can be held responsible for illegal alcohol service. This includes serving minors or intoxicated individuals. Even one mistake can lead to penalties or job loss. Understanding your role helps you make safer decisions. Consistent compliance protects both you and your employer.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Always follow your establishment’s policies. These policies are designed to meet legal requirements. Ignoring them can increase your risk. Policies also provide clear steps for handling difficult situations. Following them shows that you acted appropriately.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Documentation is important after certain incidents. This may include refusing service or handling a disruptive customer. Writing down what happened creates a record. This record can be used by management if needed. It also helps show that proper procedures were followed.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: When documenting, be accurate and objective. Write only what you observed and did. Avoid opinions or emotional language. Clear facts are more useful if the report is reviewed later. Good documentation supports your actions.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Complete documentation as soon as possible after the incident. Waiting too long can lead to missing details. Writing it while it is fresh improves accuracy. This helps ensure the report reflects what actually happened.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: If law enforcement or management reviews the situation, your documentation may be used. Clear records can support your decisions. They can show that you followed training and policies. This can help protect you in a legal or workplace review.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Never falsify or change documentation. Inaccurate reports can create serious problems. Honesty is critical when recording incidents. Accurate records protect your credibility and your job.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

contents.text: In this unit we will discuss the following topics:

paragraphs: Alcohol servers in South Carolina have a duty to serve responsibly at all times. This includes following state laws and workplace policies. Every drink served is a decision that can affect safety. You are expected to stay alert, observe customers, and act when needed. Responsible service helps prevent harm and protects both you and your employer from risk.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: You must verify that customers are legally allowed to drink. This means checking identification for every person before selling or serving alcohol and not making assumptions based on appearance. Serving alcohol without proper verification increases risk and can lead to serious consequences for both you and your business.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Alcohol service requires constant awareness. You must monitor how much each customer is drinking over time. Serving too quickly or ignoring consumption patterns can lead to over-service. Pay attention to pacing and customer behavior. Slowing down service when needed helps prevent intoxication and keeps situations from getting out of control.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Recognizing intoxication is part of your job. Watch for physical and behavioral signs such as slurred speech, poor coordination, or unusual behavior. These signs may appear gradually. Do not ignore early indicators. Acting sooner gives you more control and reduces the chance of a serious issue developing later in the shift.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: You are responsible for refusing service when necessary. This includes refusing alcohol to underage individuals and those who appear intoxicated. Refusal should be handled calmly and professionally. Use clear language and avoid confrontation. If the situation becomes difficult, seek support from a manager or coworker to help maintain control.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Following company policies is part of your legal responsibility. These policies are designed to support safe alcohol service and compliance with state expectations. They may include drink limits, ID procedures, and reporting requirements. Always follow them, even during busy times. Consistency helps prevent mistakes and reduces liability.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol servers also play a role in preventing harm beyond the establishment. Unsafe service can lead to accidents, injuries, or other serious incidents. Your decisions affect not only the customer but also others in the community. Staying alert and making responsible choices helps reduce these risks and keeps people safer.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Good communication is part of responsible service. Speak clearly and respectfully with customers. Set expectations early if needed. If you must slow or stop service, remain calm and professional. Your tone and approach can prevent conflict. Clear communication helps maintain control and keeps situations from escalating unnecessarily.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Managers are a key resource for alcohol servers in South Carolina. You are not expected to handle every situation alone. When a situation becomes unclear, difficult, or unsafe, involve a manager early. They provide guidance, support decisions, and help ensure compliance with laws and policies. Using your manager as a resource helps reduce risk and protects everyone involved.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Team accountability means everyone shares responsibility for safe alcohol service. Servers, bartenders, and managers must work together to follow laws and policies. If one person ignores rules, it can affect the entire business. Staying consistent as a team helps prevent mistakes. Support each other and communicate clearly to maintain safe and responsible service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: You should involve a manager when you are unsure about a customer’s age, behavior, or level of intoxication. Do not guess or take risks. A second opinion helps ensure the right decision is made. Managers are trained to handle these situations and can step in quickly to support safe outcomes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Managers also help handle difficult or confrontational situations. If a customer becomes upset or refuses to follow rules, do not argue. Stay calm and bring in a manager. This helps maintain control and prevents escalation. Managers often have more authority and experience in handling these interactions safely.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Supporting coworkers is part of team accountability. If you notice a coworker struggling with a situation, offer help or notify a manager. Do not ignore potential issues. A quick response can prevent larger problems. Everyone shares responsibility for maintaining a safe and compliant environment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Consistency is critical in alcohol service. All team members must follow the same rules every time. Inconsistent decisions confuse customers and increase risk. When everyone follows the same standards, it is easier to enforce policies and avoid conflict. Consistency also strengthens your team’s credibility.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Managers are responsible for enforcing policies and ensuring compliance. They may step in to stop service, handle incidents, or support staff decisions. Respect their direction and communicate openly. Following management guidance helps protect the business and ensures safe service practices are followed at all times.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: A strong team culture supports responsible alcohol service. This means valuing safety over sales and supporting each other’s decisions. When servers and managers work together, it creates a safer environment for customers and staff. Positive teamwork also improves confidence when making difficult service decisions.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Responsible marketing and promotion are important parts of alcohol service in South Carolina. Promotions should never encourage unsafe drinking. The goal is to attract customers while maintaining safety and compliance. Servers and staff must understand how promotions affect customer behavior. Poorly designed promotions can increase the risk of over-service and lead to unsafe situations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol promotions should support moderate and responsible consumption. Avoid anything that encourages excessive drinking or rapid consumption. Even if a promotion is allowed by your business, you must still monitor customer behavior closely. Your responsibility does not change based on a promotion. Safe service always comes first.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Marketing decisions are usually made by management, but servers still play a role. You must follow the rules of any promotion and apply them safely. If a promotion seems to create unsafe conditions, notify a manager. Do not continue serving in a way that increases risk just to meet promotional goals.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Promotions can change how customers behave. Discounts or specials may lead customers to drink faster or order more. Stay aware of these patterns. Watch for signs of intoxication and adjust your service. Slowing down service or offering alternatives helps maintain control during busy or high-risk promotions.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Responsible marketing also includes how alcohol is presented to customers. Staff should avoid language that encourages excessive drinking. Focus on quality, not quantity. Avoid statements that push customers to drink more. Your words can influence behavior, so keep communication professional and safety-focused.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: You must still refuse service when required, even during promotions. A discount or special does not change the law. Do not serve minors or intoxicated customers under any circumstance. Promotions should never override your responsibility to follow state laws and protect customer safety.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Managers are responsible for setting promotion guidelines, but servers must help enforce them. If a promotion creates confusion or conflict, involve a manager. Clear communication and teamwork help ensure promotions are carried out safely and consistently across all staff members.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Responsible promotion supports long-term business success. Unsafe promotions can lead to incidents, complaints, or legal issues. Safe practices protect customers, staff, and the business. When promotions are handled correctly, they create a positive environment without increasing risk or encouraging unsafe behavior.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Standard drink awareness helps alcohol servers understand how much alcohol a customer is actually consuming. Not all drinks are equal. Different types and sizes of drinks can contain different amounts of alcohol. Knowing what counts as a standard drink helps you monitor intake and prevent over-service. This knowledge supports safe service and better decision-making during every shift.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: A standard drink is a general way to measure alcohol content across different beverages. It allows you to compare beer, wine, and liquor on the same scale. This helps you track how much alcohol a customer has consumed, even if they are ordering different types of drinks throughout their visit.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Drink size and strength can vary widely. A large pour or a strong mixed drink may contain more than one standard drink. Do not assume that one glass equals one drink. Always consider how the drink is prepared. This is especially important when serving cocktails or specialty drinks.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Customers may not understand standard drink sizes. They may believe they have had “only a few drinks,” even when they have consumed more alcohol than they realize. It is your responsibility to track consumption based on standard drink awareness, not just the number of items ordered.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Standard drink awareness helps you pace service. If a customer is consuming alcohol quickly, you may need to slow down service. Offer water or suggest food when appropriate. Pacing helps reduce the risk of intoxication and gives the body time to process alcohol more safely.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Different customers are affected differently by alcohol. Body size, tolerance, and other factors can change how quickly someone becomes impaired. Even with standard drink awareness, you must still observe behavior. Use both drink tracking and visible signs to guide your decisions.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Standard drink knowledge also supports communication with coworkers and managers. When everyone understands drink strength and portions, it is easier to track customer consumption as a team. This improves consistency and helps prevent over-service during busy shifts or group service situations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Understanding standard drinks is part of responsible alcohol service expectations. It supports your role in recognizing intoxication and preventing unsafe situations. Combined with observation and good judgment, it helps you make better decisions throughout your shift and reduces the chance of harm.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Workplace culture sets the tone for how alcohol service is handled. In South Carolina, a strong culture supports safe, legal, and responsible service. This means following laws, respecting policies, and making decisions that protect customers and coworkers. A positive culture helps staff feel confident when making difficult service decisions, including refusing service when required.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Ethics in alcohol service means doing the right thing, even when it is not the easiest choice. This includes refusing service when necessary, following ID procedures, and avoiding shortcuts. Ethical decisions protect customers and reduce risk. Your actions reflect both your professionalism and your workplace standards.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Professional conduct means staying calm, respectful, and focused during your shift. Treat all customers fairly and consistently. Avoid arguments or emotional reactions. Your behavior influences how customers respond. Staying professional helps prevent conflict and supports a safe and controlled environment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Team accountability is a key part of workplace culture. Staff must support each other in following rules and making safe decisions. If a coworker needs help, step in or notify a manager. Ignoring unsafe behavior can lead to larger problems. Everyone shares responsibility for maintaining standards.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Workplace culture should prioritize safety over sales. Serving more alcohol should never be more important than following the law. If a situation feels unsafe, you are expected to act. Choosing not to serve is often the safest and most responsible decision you can make.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Managers play a key role in shaping workplace culture. They set expectations, enforce policies, and support staff decisions. When managers back responsible choices, it builds confidence across the team. Always communicate with management if you need guidance or support during service.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Professional conduct also includes how you communicate with coworkers. Clear and respectful communication helps prevent mistakes. Share concerns quickly and directly. This helps the team stay aligned and respond to issues before they become serious problems.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: A positive workplace culture encourages confidence in making difficult decisions. Refusing service, slowing down alcohol service, or involving a manager are all part of responsible behavior. When staff feel supported, they are more likely to act in ways that protect safety and comply with the law.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

sections.title: Unit 7 — Management Practices & Responsible Service Culture

contents.text: In this unit we will discuss the following topics:

paragraphs: South Carolina consistently reports alcohol-related crashes and DUI arrests each year. State data shows thousands of DUI charges annually, along with significant numbers of alcohol-related collisions. Many of these incidents involve preventable over-service. As a server, you play a key role in reducing these numbers. Responsible alcohol service directly impacts roadway safety and helps lower DUI-related harm across the state.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol-impaired driving contributes to a large share of serious crashes in South Carolina. A notable portion of traffic fatalities involve drivers with measurable alcohol impairment. These incidents often occur after prolonged drinking periods. Servers are in a position to interrupt this pattern. Recognizing when a guest should no longer be served can help reduce the likelihood of these outcomes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: DUI arrests in South Carolina are actively enforced by state and local law enforcement. Officers regularly patrol for impaired drivers and respond to alcohol-related incidents. These enforcement efforts aim to reduce harm. However, prevention starts before enforcement. Servers who follow proper procedures help reduce the number of impaired drivers on the road.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Sobriety checkpoints and targeted patrols are used during high-risk periods. These enforcement strategies increase visibility and deter impaired driving. Guests may encounter these checkpoints after leaving your establishment. This reinforces the importance of not over-serving. If a guest is impaired, they are at risk of being stopped and causing harm.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: DUI trends show increased risk during certain times. Late nights, weekends, and holidays often see higher alcohol consumption. These patterns lead to more impaired driving incidents. Servers must stay alert during these periods. Monitoring intake and pacing drinks becomes more important when risk is elevated.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Many DUI cases involve drivers who showed visible signs of intoxication before leaving. This highlights the importance of early action. Waiting too long to intervene increases risk. Servers must recognize signs and respond quickly. Preventing over-service is one of the most effective ways to reduce DUI incidents.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Repeat DUI offenses are a concern in South Carolina. Some individuals continue risky behavior even after prior incidents. This makes consistent service practices critical. You cannot rely on a guest to make safe choices. Your responsibility is to follow the law and your training every time.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: South Carolina requires alcohol server training to reduce risks tied to alcohol service. This includes understanding DUI trends and prevention strategies. Training helps servers make better decisions in real situations. Applying these skills supports statewide efforts to reduce alcohol-related harm and improve safety outcomes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol-related injuries and fatalities remain a serious concern in South Carolina. Many incidents involve preventable over-service. As alcohol consumption increases, so does the risk of harm. These outcomes affect not only the individual but also others nearby. Servers play a key role in prevention. Recognizing risk early and adjusting service can help reduce injuries and save lives.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Alcohol slows brain function and physical coordination. This increases the chance of accidents. Guests may trip, fall, or misjudge their surroundings. Even small levels of impairment can create risk. Monitoring behavior throughout service is critical. Taking action early helps prevent minor issues from becoming serious injuries.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Alcohol-related fatalities are often linked to impaired driving, but they also occur in other ways. Falls, violence, and unsafe environments can all lead to fatal outcomes. These situations can develop quickly. Servers must remain aware of guest behavior and surroundings to reduce risk.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Many injuries happen inside or near establishments. Crowded spaces, spills, and intoxicated guests increase the chance of accidents. A guest who loses balance or awareness can quickly become injured. Staff awareness and quick response are key to preventing harm in these situations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Risk increases when alcohol is combined with other factors. Fatigue, medications, and other substances can intensify impairment. Guests may appear more affected than expected. When behavior seems unusual, take it seriously. Slowing or stopping service can help prevent dangerous outcomes.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Alcohol can increase aggression and reduce self-control. Some guests may become loud, confrontational, or unpredictable. This creates risk for staff and others. Recognizing early warning signs allows you to act before a situation escalates into injury or violence.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Serious incidents often happen after multiple missed chances to intervene. A guest may show signs of impairment long before an injury occurs. Each decision matters. Acting early—by slowing service or refusing alcohol—can prevent harm and protect everyone involved.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: South Carolina training emphasizes preventing alcohol-related harm through responsible service. Servers must recognize intoxication and take appropriate action. These actions reduce the likelihood of injuries and fatalities. Your role supports safer environments and aligns with state training expectations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Alcohol service has a direct impact on community safety in South Carolina. When alcohol is served responsibly, risks are reduced. When it is not, problems can extend beyond the establishment. Communities are affected by noise, disturbances, and unsafe behavior. Servers play a role in shaping these outcomes. Responsible service helps protect both guests and the surrounding community environment.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Public safety concerns increase when alcohol is misused. This includes impaired driving, fights, and property damage. These incidents often begin with excessive drinking. What happens inside an establishment can carry into the community. Servers help control this risk by managing service carefully. Preventing over-service supports safer streets, neighborhoods, and shared public spaces.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Alcohol-related incidents can strain local resources. Law enforcement, emergency services, and healthcare providers may be called to respond. These responses take time and resources away from other needs. Preventing alcohol-related problems reduces this burden. Servers contribute by recognizing risk early and taking action before situations require outside intervention.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Neighborhood quality of life can be affected by alcohol-related behavior. Loud noise, late-night disruptions, and unsafe conduct can impact residents. These issues may lead to complaints or increased enforcement attention. Establishments that promote responsible service help maintain positive relationships with the community. This also supports long-term business success.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Public intoxication can create safety concerns for both the individual and others. Intoxicated individuals may be vulnerable to injury or may act unpredictably. This increases the risk of accidents or confrontations. Servers can help prevent this by monitoring guests and limiting alcohol when needed. Early action is more effective than reacting after problems develop.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Responsible alcohol service supports a safer environment for everyone. This includes guests, staff, and people outside the establishment. When servers follow proper procedures, fewer incidents occur. This reduces harm and builds trust with the community. Every decision made during service has a potential impact beyond the immediate setting.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: South Carolina emphasizes the importance of understanding community impact as part of alcohol server training. Servers are expected to recognize how their actions affect public safety. This includes preventing service to intoxicated individuals. Applying this knowledge helps reduce harm and supports safer communities statewide.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Working as a team improves community safety outcomes. Staff should communicate about guest behavior and potential risks. Managers can provide support when situations escalate. A coordinated response helps prevent incidents from spreading beyond the establishment. Strong teamwork creates a safer environment for both guests and the public.

paragraphs: Law enforcement plays a key role in overseeing alcohol service in South Carolina. Agencies monitor establishments to ensure laws are followed. This includes preventing sales to minors and intoxicated individuals. Oversight helps protect public safety and maintain accountability. Servers should expect that enforcement is active and ongoing. Understanding this helps reinforce the importance of following proper procedures every shift.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Compliance checks are used to verify that alcohol laws are being followed. These checks may occur without warning. They are designed to test whether staff follow ID procedures and service rules. Servers must treat every transaction as if it is being observed. Consistent behavior is critical. Following the same steps every time helps ensure compliance during inspections.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Law enforcement agencies may conduct routine visits to licensed establishments. These visits help ensure that operations meet legal requirements. Officers may observe service, review procedures, or address complaints. These interactions are part of normal oversight. Staying professional and cooperative during these visits is important for maintaining compliance.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Underage compliance operations are a common enforcement method. These checks test whether staff properly verify identification. Failing to check ID or making assumptions can lead to violations. Servers must always follow ID procedures, regardless of how confident they feel. Consistency is key to passing these checks and avoiding penalties.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Oversight also includes monitoring service to intoxicated individuals. Law enforcement may observe whether staff recognize and respond to impairment. Serving an intoxicated guest increases risk and can lead to enforcement action. Servers must stay alert and act when necessary. Preventing over-service supports both compliance and safety.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Failure to follow alcohol laws can result in serious consequences. These may include fines, legal action, or business penalties. Enforcement actions can affect both the individual server and the establishment. Understanding these risks reinforces the need for consistent compliance. Every decision during service can have legal impact.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Managers play an important role during enforcement interactions. They provide guidance and support to staff. If a situation becomes unclear, involving a manager is the right step. Team coordination helps ensure proper responses. This also demonstrates professionalism during inspections or compliance checks.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: South Carolina requires alcohol server training to include awareness of enforcement and compliance expectations. Servers must understand their responsibilities and apply them consistently. This helps reduce violations and supports lawful operations. Training prepares you to handle real-world enforcement situations with confidence.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.
Click each item to learn more.

paragraphs: Real-world cases show how alcohol service decisions impact safety. In many DUI crash reports, the driver had been drinking at a licensed establishment before the incident. These cases highlight missed chances to intervene. Servers are often the last point of contact before a guest drives. Learning from these examples helps you recognize risk and act earlier in similar situations.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: In one reported case, a visibly impaired guest continued to be served over time. Staff did not intervene. The guest later caused a serious crash. This situation reflects a failure to recognize and act on clear warning signs. Consistent monitoring and timely refusal of service can prevent these outcomes and protect both the guest and the public.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: Another case involved an underage individual who was served alcohol without proper ID verification. The guest later experienced alcohol poisoning. Proper ID procedures protect both the guest and the server.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: In some cases, intoxicated guests have been involved in fights inside establishments. These situations often begin with minor signs of agitation that go unnoticed. Without intervention, behavior can escalate quickly. Staff awareness and early involvement can prevent injuries and maintain a safe environment for all guests.
text.instruction: Read the following, then click Next.

paragraphs: A common pattern in these cases is delayed response. Staff may notice warning signs but hesitate to act. This delay increases risk. Taking action early—such as slowing service or involving a manager—can prevent situations from escalating. Confidence and consistency are key to effective intervention.

paragraphs: Real-world outcomes often affect more than just the individual involved. Injuries, property damage, and legal consequences can impact many people.
